raw.reasoning: “March 4” most naturally refers to March 4 in the anchor year 2026, though a different year is possible if omitted.
Because the anchor date is Tuesday and 3pm is later the same day, the most likely interpretation is today at 3pm, though next Tuesday is a plausible alternative.
With only “March 4” and an anchor in 2026, the most likely concrete date is March 4, 2026, though March 4 in another year such as 2027 is also plausible.
With the anchor at 9:00 AM on 2025-09-15, '5pm' clearly refers to 5:00 PM on the same day.
“The 1st” is a temporal expression but lacks a month or direction, so I chose the 1st day of the anchor month while other dates such as the next 1st are equally plausible.
“The 5th” most likely refers to the next upcoming 5th of the month from the anchor date, which is November 5, 2025, at noon.
“In two weeks” clearly means two weeks after the anchor time, yielding 2026-03-13 at 15:20 in America/New_York.
“A month ago” is resolved by subtracting one calendar month from the anchor time, yielding December 15, 2025 at 10:00 in America/New_York.
“A month ago” most directly means the same local time one calendar month before the anchor, though it could also be interpreted more loosely as sometime in the previous month.
“A year ago” relative to the anchor time is exactly one calendar year earlier, preserving the same local time.
“Three days ago” most naturally refers to the calendar date three days before the anchor date, though it could also mean exactly 72 hours earlier.
“In 3 days” from the anchor time of 2025-12-30T13:00:00-05:00 resolves clearly to the same time on 2026-01-02.

Counting three business days after the anchor and excluding the Thanksgiving holiday on 2025-11-27 gives Tuesday 2025-12-02, though some interpretations might count only weekdays and land on 2025-12-01.
“30 days ago” clearly refers to the instant exactly 30 days before the anchor time.
“In 6 months” clearly refers to the point six calendar months after the anchor time, preserving the local time in America/New_York.
With the anchor date Thursday 2026-03-12, the day after tomorrow is Saturday 2026-03-14.
Counting back five business days from Monday 2025-09-15, excluding the weekend, lands on Monday 2025-09-08.
“A month ago” is resolved as one calendar month before the anchor time, preserving the same local time.
“In 3 days” is resolved as exactly three days after the anchor time, preserving the same local time in America/New_York.
Six weeks before the anchor time of 2026-01-15T10:00:00-05:00 is exactly 2025-12-04T10:00:00-05:00.
“Two months from now” clearly means the same time of day two calendar months after the anchor time.
“In three days” clearly means three days after the anchor time, preserving the same local time.
Interpreted “this week” as the current Sunday-start week in America/New_York containing the anchor date, though a Monday-start week would instead begin on 2026-04-13.
“Memorial Day” most likely refers to the U.S. holiday in the anchor year 2026, which falls on the last Monday of May, May 25, though a past or future year could also be intended.
With the 2025 anchor, “Labor Day” most likely refers to the U.S. Labor Day in the current year, which was Monday, 2025-09-01, though no year was explicitly stated.
“YTD” clearly means year-to-date, from the start of the current calendar year through the anchor time.
With the anchor on Sunday, “last week” most likely means the previous calendar week in a Sunday-start convention, April 12–18, though a Monday-start week could instead point to April 6–12.
“MTD” means month-to-date, so it runs from the start of the current month to the anchor time.
“YTD” means year-to-date, from the start of the current calendar year through the anchor time.
“This month” clearly refers to the current calendar month containing the anchor date, March 2026.
With no year specified, the most likely interpretation is the upcoming U.S. Thanksgiving after the anchor date, though the previous or another year's Thanksgiving is possible.
“Last week” most likely refers to the previous calendar week before the anchor date, interpreted as Monday through Sunday, though a Sunday-start week is also plausible.
Using the org calendar all_hands definition, the next last-Friday monthly 15:00–16:00 event on or after the anchor is September 26, 2025.
Using the org-defined next_billing_cycle after the current 2025-12-15 through 2026-01-14 cycle, the next billing cycle runs from January 15 through February 14, 2026.
“Open enrollment” matches the org calendar period of November 1–15 inclusive, and with no qualifier the next occurrence after the 2026-03-12 anchor is the clearest choice.
The org-defined next maintenance_window is the next 2nd Saturday 02:00–06:00 local on or after the anchor time, which is March 14, 2026.
“This quarter” relative to the Jan 15, 2026 anchor refers to the current calendar quarter, Q1 2026.
“The year” most likely refers to the current year from the anchor, so the first half is January 1 through June 30, 2026.
“Q1” most likely refers to the first calendar quarter of the anchor year 2026, though a fiscal-year interpretation is possible.
“The next three days” most naturally means the 72-hour period starting from the anchor time, though it could also mean the next three calendar days.
With the anchor in March 2026, “weekends in March” most clearly refers to Saturdays and Sundays in March 2026, though it could refer to another March if additional context existed.
“Weekdays this week” clearly refers to Monday through Friday of the current week containing the anchor date, March 9–13, 2026.
With the anchor in March 2026, “since March” most naturally means from the start of March 2026 onward, though it could refer to another March if additional context existed.
“Since March” most likely means starting at the beginning of March in the current year relative to the anchor, with an open-ended end.
Interpreted as the first seven days of March in the anchor year 2026, though some conventions might treat the first week as the first Monday-starting week.
“The last 7 days” most commonly means the rolling 7-day period ending now, though it could also mean the last seven calendar days.
With no year specified and the anchor within March 2026, the range most clearly refers to March 1 through March 15 of 2026, interpreted as inclusive of March 15 via a half-open end on March 16.
“Q1” indicates a first quarter but does not specify a year or fiscal calendar, so the upcoming calendar Q1 after the anchor is chosen as the best guess.
“June onward” most naturally means starting at the beginning of June in the anchor year and continuing indefinitely, though it could also be read as starting now within June.
With the anchor in March 2026, “March 1 through 4” most likely refers to March 1–4, 2026 inclusive, represented as a half-open range ending March 5.
With only the month name given, the most likely concrete choice is the upcoming December relative to the June 2026 anchor, though another year could be intended.
With the anchor on Tuesday 2026-06-09, “this Monday” and “this Friday” clearly refer to Monday and Friday of the current week.
With the anchor date in June 2026, “this month” is June 2026 and “last month” is May 2026.
“This week” is most naturally the Monday–Sunday week containing the anchor date, and Thanksgiving in 2025 is Thursday 2025-11-27, though a Sunday-start week is also plausible.
With the anchor in March 2026, “in March and in May” most naturally refers to March 2026 and May 2026, though the year is not explicitly stated.
With the anchor in 2026, “Q2 this year” is April through June 2026 and “Q2 last year” is April through June 2025.
“Last week” is most naturally the previous Monday–Friday workweek before the Thursday anchor, with two daily blocks of 8–12 and 1–5.
June 1 is most likely June 1, 2026 from the anchor year, and “the week after” most likely means the following calendar week, though it could also mean the seven days immediately after June 1.
“Yesterday” relative to the anchor is 2026-06-08, and the two specified time windows are unambiguous.
“This month” refers to March 2026 from the anchor date, and excluding weekends leaves the weekday spans Monday through Friday.

“Past month” is resolved as the rolling month ending at the anchor time, with Tue–Thu 8am–12pm windows included and the current Tuesday window truncated at now, though last calendar month is also plausible.
I interpreted “the last 30 days” as the 30 calendar-day window ending on the anchor date, inclusive of today, and “business days” as weekdays only without applying a holiday calendar.
“Current” refers directly to the anchor time, so it resolves to now.
“Latest” indicates the most recent report available as of the anchor time, but it does not specify a concrete report date or time window.
“Next month” clearly refers to April 2026 from the anchor date, while “early” is approximate and is interpreted as the first ten days of the month.
With the anchor on Tuesday, “the weekend” most likely refers to the upcoming Saturday–Sunday, though the previous weekend is also plausible without more context.
“Recently” is highly vague, so I chose the last 30 days ending at the anchor time as a conventional concrete interpretation.
“Mid-month” most likely refers to the middle of the current month, June 2026, commonly approximated as the 15th, though it could also mean a broader middle-of-month period.
“The last few months” most likely means roughly the past three months up to the anchor time, though it could also mean the last several complete calendar months.
“End of year” most likely refers to the last calendar day of the current year, 2025, though it could also mean a broader late-December period or a fiscal year end.
“This afternoon” most likely refers to the afternoon of the anchor date, though the exact afternoon boundaries are somewhat conventional.
“Soon” indicates a near-future time relative to the anchor, but its exact duration is highly context-dependent, so I interpreted it as roughly the next week.
With the anchor on Dec 30, 2025, “the new year” most likely refers to the upcoming calendar year 2026, though it could also mean New Year’s Day specifically.
“Around the holidays” is vague, but in the U.S. context near the anchor date it most likely refers to the winter holiday season from Thanksgiving through New Year’s.
“Over the summer” most likely refers to the upcoming summer season from June through August 2026, though astronomical summer or another contextual summer period is also possible.
From Tuesday 2026-06-09, “next Friday” most commonly refers to the upcoming Friday, 2026-06-12, though some people might mean the Friday of the following week.
“About a month ago” most directly resolves to roughly one month before the anchor date of 2026-03-12, i.e. 2026-02-12, though the word “about” allows nearby dates.

Given the anchor is the day before U.S. Thanksgiving, “the long weekend” most likely refers to the upcoming Thanksgiving long weekend starting on 2025-11-27, though some might consider it starting Friday.
